title: How Python Runs Code
subtitle: Understand the interpreter and learn to read error messages
learning_objectives: Explain how Python reads and runs code line by line using an interpreter
Understand what a SyntaxError is and what causes one
Understand what a NameError is and what causes one
Read a Python error message to find the type, line number, and description

sections.heading: Python Reads Your Code One Line at a Time
sections.content:
When you run a Python program, Python doesn't read the whole thing at once and then run it. Instead, it uses an interpreter — a program that reads your code one line at a time, from top to bottom, and runs each line immediately before moving to the next one.

This is different from some other languages that require a separate compilation step first. With Python, you write code and run it instantly — no waiting.

Because Python runs line by line, if it hits a problem on line 3, it will have already successfully run lines 1 and 2. It will stop at line 3 and report an error. This makes it a little easier to find where things went wrong.
sections.code_example:
print("Line 1: Python runs this first")
print("Line 2: Then this")
print("Line 3: Then this")
print("Line 4: And finally this")
sections.code_output:
Line 1: Python runs this first
Line 2: Then this
Line 3: Then this
Line 4: And finally this
sections.note: The order of your code matters! Python always starts at the top and works its way down, line by line.

sections.heading: When Things Go Wrong: Reading Error Messages
sections.content:
Every programmer — even experienced ones — makes mistakes. When Python encounters a problem, it stops and shows you an error message (also called a traceback). Learning to read these messages is one of the most important skills you can develop.

An error message tells you three key things:
  1. The line number — where in your code the problem was found
  2. The error type — what kind of mistake it is
  3. A description — a short explanation of what went wrong

SyntaxError — This means you made a typo in the structure of the code itself. For example, forgetting a closing parenthesis or a quotation mark. Python can't even understand what you meant to write.

NameError — This means you used a name (like a variable or function) that Python doesn't recognize. This usually happens when you misspell a name or try to use something before it has been defined.
sections.code_example:
# Example of a SyntaxError (missing closing quote and parenthesis):
# print("Hello)
# Python would say: SyntaxError: EOL while scanning string literal

# Example of a NameError (using a name that doesn't exist):
# print(mesage)  # 'mesage' was never defined
# Python would say: NameError: name 'mesage' is not defined

# Correct version:
print("Hello")
message = "Everything is fine!"
print(message)
sections.note: Error messages are your friends! They tell you exactly what went wrong and where to look. Don't be scared of them — use them as clues.
sections.warning: When you see an error, always check the line number Python mentions first. Sometimes the real mistake is on the line just above the one reported — especially with SyntaxErrors.

exercises.title: Line-by-Line Output
exercises.prompt:
Write a program with exactly 3 print() statements that run one after another. Print these three lines in order:
1. Python reads top to bottom.
2. Each line runs in order.
3. That is how the interpreter works!
exercises.solution:
print("Python reads top to bottom.")
print("Each line runs in order.")
print("That is how the interpreter works!")
hints: You need three separate print() calls, one per line.
The order you write them is the order they will appear in the output.
Example first line: print("Python reads top to bottom.")

tests.message: Output contains: Python reads top to bottom.
Output contains: Each line runs in order.

tests.value: That is how the interpreter works!
tests.message: Output contains: That is how the interpreter works!

exercises.title: Fix the Broken Code
exercises.prompt:
The code below has two mistakes — a NameError waiting to happen and a variable used correctly. Your job is to fix it so it runs without errors and prints: My favorite language is Python!

Here is the broken code to fix:

print(favoriteLanguage)
exercises.starter_code:
# Fix this code so it prints: My favorite language is Python!
# Hint: the variable favoriteLanguage is used but never defined!
# You need to define it first, then print it.

# Create a variable called favoriteLanguage and assign "Python!" to it
# Print the message using the variable
exercises.solution:
favoriteLanguage = "Python!"
print("My favorite language is", favoriteLanguage)
hints: A NameError means you are using a name that Python doesn't know yet.
You need to give favoriteLanguage a value before you use it.
Try: favoriteLanguage = "Python!" on the line before the print statement.

quiz.question: What kind of error does Python show when you misspell the structure of the code itself — for example, forgetting a closing quote?
options: NameError
TypeError
SyntaxError
RunError
quiz.explanation: A SyntaxError means Python cannot understand the structure of what you wrote — like a grammar mistake in human language. Forgetting a closing quote, parenthesis, or colon are common causes.

quiz.question: You see this error: NameError: name 'colour' is not defined. What most likely caused it?
options: Python does not support color-related code
You used the name 'colour' before defining it, or you misspelled it
You forgot a closing parenthesis somewhere
The number on that line is too large
quiz.explanation: A NameError means you used a name that Python doesn't recognize. Either the variable was never created, or it was misspelled. For example, if you defined 'color' (without a 'u') but then tried to use 'colour', Python would report a NameError.

quiz.question: Python uses an interpreter. What does that mean?
options: Python translates the whole program to another language before running it
Python reads and runs your code one line at a time, from top to bottom
Python requires a special app to be installed before code can run
Python checks all the code for errors before running any of it
quiz.explanation: Python's interpreter reads your code one line at a time and runs each line immediately before moving to the next. This means you can run code instantly without a separate compilation step.

quiz.question: If Python encounters an error on line 5 of your program, what happens to lines 1 through 4?
options: Python cancels everything, including lines 1 to 4
Python skips to line 6 and continues
Lines 1 to 4 have already run — Python stops at line 5
Python reruns the whole program from line 1
quiz.explanation: Because Python runs code line by line from top to bottom, lines 1 through 4 will have already executed before Python reaches the error on line 5. It stops at line 5 and reports the error.

quiz.question: What three things does a Python error message tell you?
options: The file size, the date, and the programmer's name
The line number, the error type, and a description of what went wrong
The correct code, the wrong code, and a suggested fix
The memory used, the time taken, and the output
quiz.explanation: A Python error message always tells you: (1) the line number where the problem was found, (2) the type of error (like SyntaxError or NameError), and (3) a short description of what went wrong. Use these three clues to fix your code.

completion_message: Excellent! Understanding how Python runs code — and how to read error messages — is a superpower that will save you countless hours as you keep learning. Errors are not failures, they are helpful clues. You are well on your way!
